paragraphs:
The People of Egypt

Origin of the Egyptians
The Egyptians had a unique origin, characterized by various phenomena in their language and physical type. Marked Varieties of Physique
There are two notable types of physique among the Egyptians. The Hebrews adopted the concept, and although they occasionally referred to Egypt as "Mazor" in the singular, they more frequently used the dual form "Mizraim," meaning "the two lands." "The two Mazors," "two Egypts," or "two lands" referred to the blossom and the stalk. The broad tract on the Mediterranean was known as "Lower Egypt," or "the Delta." To the south lay a long, narrow valley, like a green snake, bearing the name of "Upper Egypt," or "the Sudan." "There is no greater contrast than that between these two regions." If the true Egypt is the land we have described—the Nile valley, including the Fayoum and the Delta—the lily plant, its bud, and its bloom—we can reasonably explain why it was said in ancient times that "Egypt was a gift from the river." The Greek philosopher who first used the term "divine" in a scientific context did not necessarily understand its full implications.

The land thus left exposed is Egypt. It is the temporarily uncovered bed of the Nile, which it reclaims and recovers during a portion of each year. During this time, Egypt disappears from view, except where human labour has created artificial islands by mounds and embankments, raising their heads above the waste of waters. These islands are for the most part crowned with buildings. "Hail to you, O Nile." The river's majesty shines forth. Though the Nile is useful, beneficent, and indeed essential to the existence of Egypt, it cannot be said that it adds much to the variety of the landscape or to the beauty of the scenery.

The land was deeply scarred by the floodwaters, which had carried down rich, reddish-brown soil from the highlands of Abyssinia. Athens, Sparta, Rhodes, Genoa, Florence, and Venice were great states. The rich alluvium continually grew deeper and was annually replenished by nature's generous hand. It possessed boundless fertility and yielded a triple harvest every year: first a grain crop, followed by two successive crops of grasses or edible vegetables.

Wheat, however, was throughout antiquity the chief product of Egypt, which was considered the granary of the world. The country served as a refuge and resource for its neighbouring nations during times of scarcity. In later republican and imperial periods, Rome relied almost entirely on Egypt for its sustenance. Northwesterly winds prevail in Egypt throughout the summer and autumn. By raising a sail, it is usually possible to navigate upstream at a moderate pace. Nature's operations are, however, so uniform that such calamities rarely occur.

The summers are hot, especially in the south, where an occasional sirocco brings intense discomfort while it lasts. However, the cool Etesian wind blowing from the north helps temper the sun's rays even in the hottest season of the year. During the remaining months, from October to April, the climate is delightful. Egypt has two distinct seasons: spring and summer.

The fabric was excellent, as demonstrated by its continued use to this day, and by the fact that the Greeks and Romans, after extensive testing, preferred it to parchment. In the past, a large population of gazelles drove away the entire antelope tribe, which was confined to the desert region beyond the alluvial plain.

The crocodile, however, was not hunted by the ancient Egyptians. The hippopotamus was also hunted by them. They further indulged their love of sport in the pursuits of fowling and fishing. A single broad river, unbroken within the limits of Egypt, even by a rapid strip of green plain at its side. Beyond it lie two low lines of straight-topped hills, and a boundless open space where the river divides itself into six sluggish branches before reaching the sea. This vast expanse constitutes Egypt, which is by nature a southern land---"weary, stale, flat and unprofitable." The monotony is alleviated, however, in two ways, and by two causes. The olive afterglow gradually gave way to a deep blue-grey hue.

Monkeys are not found everywhere; we have no evidence that they were ever indigenous in Egypt, although as pets, they were very common among the Egyptians, who delighted in keeping them. The two types of faces do not differ significantly in frame. Both these and the other sacred animals were carefully embalmed after death, and their bodies were buried in sacred repositories. The courtier fell before the king, overcome with amazement. The king's favor. After four successive rulers, each holding power for many years, Osiris, the son of Geb and Nut, ascended to the throne and ruled over both the Upper and Lower Regions.

He had done this after a while. Having placed the body in a coffin, he then threw it into the Nile, where it floated downstream to the sea. Horus then renewed the war with his uncle. He ultimately defeated him with a long spear, driving it into his skull. The gods and goddesses of the Osirid legend, including Seb, Nut, Osiris, Isis, Nephthys, Set, and Horus, were those that most drew towards them the thoughts of the Egyptians. The greater number of these deities were favourite objects of worship, while Set was generally detested. The complex polytheism of the monuments and the literature was not the practical religion of many Egyptians.

All Egyptians shared a common belief, whether it was in the literal concept of an immortal soul or not, but certainly in the existence of a life beyond death, followed by a judgment that would assess each individual based on their actions during their lifetime on earth. The soul descended into the Lower World and was taken to the Hall of Truth, where it was judged by Osiris and the 42 assessors, known as the Lords of Truth. Anubis, "the director of the weight," brought forth a pair of scales. He placed a figure or emblem of Truth in one scale and a vase containing the good actions of the deceased in the other. Thoth stood by, holding a tablet to record the result.

If no text is provided, I will return nothing. No correction needed. The good soul, having first been cleansed of its impurities by passing through the basin of purgatorial fire guarded by four genii, was made the companion of Osiris for three thousand years. Afterward, it returned from Amenti and re-entered its former body to live once more as a human on earth. My soul is pure; while I lived, I bore no ill will.

Men of the future will be charmed by my merits. "I have not oppressed any widow; no prisoner languished in my days; no one died of hunger." It has been said that Egypt never produced more than one myth, the Osirid legend. This is indeed true, as the story is rarely told at any considerable length or with a significant number of exciting incidents. The Egyptians left behind many brief legends, some of which retain a modicum of interest. These stories suggest that the ancient people were not entirely lacking in creativity, although their imagination was limited.

The quest for unlawful knowledge involved all who sought it in calamity. They claimed that mankind must be destroyed, and entrusted the task of destruction to Athor and Sekhet, who went on to smite men across the entire land. The force of geographic fact proved too strong to be permanently overcome, and after a few centuries, power gravitated back to the centre indicated by nature.

Some ruins of the fortifications are still visible; and the mines themselves, now exhausted, pierce the sides of the rocks, bearing in many places traces of hieroglyphical inscriptions. She was regarded as man's true "helpmate," sharing his thoughts and ruling his family. During their early years, she had charge of his children. In ancient Egypt, polygamy was not practiced during the primitive period; even kings had only one wife. After many nights of fantasy, after many days of deep desire,
I lay upon the desert's edge, rejoicing as an ancient hermit. Before me rose a wonderful array: those works where man has rivalled Nature most. Those Pyramids stood fearlessly against decay,
like waves that crash upon the rockiest coast,
or winds that boast endurance on mountain-steeps. They were required to be, and in most cases are, of good construction throughout.

The first passage was carried through the substance of the pyramid for a distance of approximately 110 feet at a descending angle of 25 degrees. After the 55th stage, it became horizontal and was tunnelled through the native rock on which the pyramid was built. It began with a steep descent. It was forty feet long, which is equivalent to a hundred feet; then it became horizontal for fifty feet. After that, it rose gently for ninety-six feet and joined the first passageway roughly halfway between the sepulchral chamber and the outside air. The tradition that attributed it to the immediate predecessor of Men-kau-ra can be considered sufficient evidence for its authorship.

If its cubic contents were to be built, it would create a city with 22,000 houses similar to those described above. Laid out in a line of cubic squares, this arrangement would span nearly 17,000 miles or encircle two-thirds of the Earth's circumference at the equator. The bulk of the interior is composed of relatively small stones, which are carefully cut and fitted together to form a compact whole. Above these blocks is a low chamber, similarly covered. This is repeated four times. Afterward, there is a fifth opening, triangular, with a roof supported by a set of huge sloping blocks that meet at the apex and support one another.

If anything is necessary, we may add that every pyramid in Egypt was built for the same purpose. There are more than sixty of them. They all occupy sites in the great necropolis, or burial ground, opposite Memphis, where the inhabitants are known to have laid their dead. In this case, there would have been nothing ideal about the conception of Khufu. He would simply have happened to erect the largest pyramid because he happened to have the longest reign. However, except for the "Third Pyramid," there is a unity of design in the structures, which implies that the architect had conceived the whole structure in his mind from the beginning. We must hold that Khufu conceived a design unlike any other—required his architect to build a tomb that would surpass all previous monuments and be difficult to match.

Life and property were alike insecure. There was no stability of fortune for either the ignorant or the learned man. One night, after he lay down to sleep, he was suddenly attacked in his bedchamber. The sound of clashing arms echoed loudly nearby.

He waged wars against the Petti, bowmen of the Libyan interior, the Sakti or Asiatics, the Maxyes or Mazyes of the north-west, and the Ua-uat and other negro tribes of the south; not, however, as it would seem, with any desire to make conquests, but solely for the protection of his own frontier. Of all its glories, nothing now remains but one tapering obelisk of pink granite, which rises into the soft, sleepy air above the green cornfields of Matariyeh. No longer tipped with gold, it still catches the earliest and latest sun-rays. Wild bees nestle in the crannies of the weird characters cut into the stone. Usurtasen, after reigning for ten years alongside his father and thirty-one years alone, associated his son, Amenemhat II. He became the sole king about three years later.

Thirty-seven people, including men, women, and children, gathered before the great noble's court near the eastern border. They offered their homage and requested a favorable hearing. Usurtasen grew to be a giant over seven feet tall, who conquered not only Ethiopia but also Europe and Asia. His columns were said to have been found in Palestine, Asia Minor, Scythia, and Thrace. He left a colony at Colchis, the city of the golden fleece. Usurtasen dug all the canals that intersected Egypt. He invented geometry. He erected colossi above 50 feet high. He was the greatest monarch to rule Egypt since the days of Osiris. Nature's mechanism is remarkably consistent, with annual flooding varying only slightly over time. The event has remained largely unchanged since ancient times, when the first Pharaoh built a temple on the banks of the Nile at Memphis. Despite this consistency, there are still variations that make the phenomenon remarkable and awe-inspiring.

Disasters of this kind are, however, exceedingly rare. When they occur, their results are terrible to contemplate. Only the lands closest to the river have been inundated. Those farther away remain dry and barren throughout the summer, failing to produce even a single blade of grass or a single ear of corn. The good king was not content with simply benefiting his people by increasing Egypt's productivity and protecting the land from occasional calamities. He also provided employment for large numbers, which was neither severe nor oppressive, but rather promoted their comfort and well-being.

He built a new type of building in Fayoum that was later admired for its uniqueness, even surpassing the pyramids. It was with much reluctance that he left the open downs and fresh breezes and oak groves of Canaan, a land promised to him and his seed after him. He took his way through the desert of the south to the great kingdom with which he and his race could never hope to have solid friendship.

It is impossible to put into words the extent to which Egypt suffered over the course of these centuries due to the upheaval of its past. As they developed settled habits and assimilated with their subjects, they started building temples to him, following the Egyptian model, in their main towns. After the dynasty had ruled for five reigns, spanning approximately 150 years, a king named Apepi ascended to the throne. Joseph's arrival did not coincide with the period of the Old Empire, unlike Abraham's. The presence of horses, chariots, wagons, and carts during Joseph's time suggests that these technologies had been introduced after the Hyksos invasion. The Pharaoh of Joseph is evidently resident in Lower Egypt, not at Thebes, which was the seat of government for many years both before and after the Hyksos rule.

If we consider placing Joseph under one of the "Shepherd Kings," it is logical to accept the tradition linking him to Apepi. It seems strange at first sight that the terrible warriors who, under Set or Saites, so easily reduced Egypt to subjection, and then still further weakened the population by massacre and oppression, were eventually gotten rid of after only two centuries. The Elamites, Babylonians, Assyrians, Medes, and Persians successively dominated Western Asia, with each culture weakening and exhausting over varying periods due to similar factors.

The people acknowledged the Hyksos monarchs as their suzerains in a certain sense, indicating their subjection through annual tribute payments. However, they retained their own native princes, administration, government, religion, and laws. Unlike the newcomers, they did not live among them intermixed. The fact that they paid tribute did not prevent them from preserving their self-respect, resulting in neither moral nor physical deterioration. It would seem that they could have engaged in wars independently with the peoples living upstream on the Nile, or with the desert tribes, thereby maintaining a warlike culture among themselves as the Hyksos became less accustomed to it. The Ra-Sekenens of Thebes, who referred to themselves as "great" and "very great," likely built significant power in Upper Egypt during the reigns of the later "Shepherd Kings." They improved their military system by adopting the horse and chariot, which the Hyksos had introduced. Through training their people in arms, they earned a reputation as skilled warriors.

In the long negotiation between the two parties, as recorded in the "First Sallier Papyrus," it is evident that Ra-Sekenen has committed no act for which Apepi has any right to complain. However, this has awakened feelings of such hostility in Ra-Sekenen that Apepi will be content with nothing less than either unconditional submission to every demand he chooses to make or war on a full scale. He was finally forced to take up arms. He gathered a force of approximately 500,000 men and simultaneously deployed a fleet of ships on the Nile, which proved invaluable in his subsequent campaigns.

The fleet was ordered to attack the walls on the side of the lagoon. The land force was engaged in battering the defenses elsewhere. Assaults were made day after day with only partial success. However, the defenders eventually became exhausted—a panic set in, and they hastily evacuated the area, retreating towards Syria, their original point of departure. He brought back a considerable amount of wealth and a large number of Asiatic captives to Egypt.

The cloistered court was a notable architectural feature, becoming increasingly common after its initial appearance. It was not until after the death of her father that she came forward prominently and assumed a position previously held by no other female in Egypt, except possibly Nitocris.

Over the course of six years, four kings occupied the Babylonian throne, with two meeting violent ends. Yet Amasis appears to have been content and at peace, enjoying a life that was more carefree and enjoyable than most monarchs', without attempting to challenge or overthrow the Babylonian supremacy or restore Egypt's independence. About B C Ten years after Nebuchadnezzar's subjection of Egypt, Cyrus, son of Cambyses, took on an independent role as the tributary monarch of Persia under the Medes, beginning a career of conquest. C The Persian Empire, under King Cyrus the Great, had conquered a vast territory that stretched from modern-day Iran to Egypt. The Persian monarch noted and resented the interference with territories he considered his own. It is unclear whether he took any steps to avenge himself.

Cyrus did not march in person against Amasis because of the other wars that had taken place during his remaining years. The conquest of the nations between the Caspian and the Indian Ocean delayed him. A danger then emerged on his north-eastern frontier, requiring all his attention, and it was this that ultimately led to his demise. The troops of Psamatik fled after a brief struggle, and soon their retreat had become a complete rout.

Defeated commanders have not always seen this, and those who do see it allow prudence to be overpowered by other considerations. The Persian army quickly surrounded the city, cutting it off from the outside world. The city fell shortly after its investment. The monarch, enraged by the event, punished the perpetrators by publicly executing 2,000 of the city's most prominent citizens, reportedly including the son of the deposed ruler.

Cambyses conformed to Egyptian usages by taking a "throne-name," following the pattern of ancient Pharaohs. He cleared the temple of Neith at Sais from foreign occupants and entrusted its care to an Egyptian officer of high standing. Additionally, he was initiated into the mysteries of the goddess herself. He was not always in a state of excitement; he enjoyed periods of lucidity, during which he was guided by the spirit of an administrator and a statesman.

He failed to alleviate the discontent, and even hatred, that the outrages of Cambyses had stirred; they still lingered indelibly in the Egyptian mind; the Persian rule was detested; and in sullen dissatisfaction, the entire nation waited for an opportunity to reclaim its independence and cast off the accursed yoke. Egypt, after making secret preparations for three years, launched a surprise attack on Britain in 1942. C  She probably overpowered and massacred the Persian garrison in Memphis. The city was said to have been defended by 120,000 men. Proclaiming her independence, she established a native sovereign.

C Xerxes, the son and successor of 486, immediately undertook the suppression of this event. The following year, he invaded Egypt and easily crushed all resistance. He then placed the province under a more severe rule than it had ever known before. Twenty-five years of peace passed, during which the Egyptians were submissive subjects of the Persian Empire. Mardonius also placed great value on the marines who fought on the Egyptian ships. He retained them as land troops when the Persian fleet returned to Asia after the Battle of Salamis.

No further defection took place during the reign of Xerxes; but in 460 B.C., after the throne had been occupied for about five years by Xerxes' son, Artaxerxes, a second rebellion broke out, which led to a long and terrible struggle. Memphis, however, the capital, still resisted, and the struggle thus remained doubtful. Meanwhile, Artaxerxes was not idle.

Megabyzus besieged them without success for eighteen months. However, he eventually devised a plan similar to the one by which Cyrus had captured Babylon, and implemented it accordingly. The entire fleet of 200 vessels fell into the hands of the conqueror. Fifty others, sent as a reinforcement, soon entered the river and were attacked without warning, suffering heavy losses. Amyrtaeus, who had been recognized as king of Egypt, took refuge in the Nile marshes, where he endured a miserable existence.

He traveled through the land as far as Elephantine, observing with keen eyes the wonders depicted in "The Story of Egypt," and he described them with the enthusiasm that we had previously observed. We only know that after a time, the power of Nefaa-rut was established; Persia left him in undisturbed possession of Egypt, and he reigned quietly for six years, employing himself in the repair and restoration of the temple of Ammon at Karnak.

A period of independence ensued, lasting approximately sixty years. This marked a respite from the monarchy's decline, briefly reviving memories of past glories. The fear of re-conquest was also perpetual. As a result, monarchs were constantly seeking alliances with foreign states, exposing themselves to risks that could have been avoided with greater prudence. He applied himself with considerable success to the revival of Egyptian art and architecture during this time.

The art of his time was said to possess the elegance of that produced under the 26th (Psamatik) dynasty, but was somewhat more florid. It is not surprising that Nectanebo was worshipped after his death as a divine being. His own hold on Egypt, however, was too weak to justify such a bold proceeding.

The Regent, to whom he had entrusted the direction of affairs during his absence, proved unfaithful. He then incited his son, Nekht-nebf, to become a candidate for the throne and take up arms against his father. The rebels' party quickly regained strength after their defeats. Nectanebo II Having established himself firmly on the throne after a period of time, he eliminated pretenders and resumed the ambitious policy of his predecessor. He then formed an alliance with the people of Sidon and their neighbors, who were in revolt against Persia.

The success, however, which was thus gained by the rebels, only served to exasperate the Persian king, who resolved all the more on a desperate endeavor. The assistance thus secured was numerically small, amounting to no more than ten thousand men—a thirtieth part of his native force. However, it formed the core of the force that relied on Greek mercenaries from Egypt, who joined him later, and this core proved crucial to the ultimate success of his expedition. The two in conjunction betrayed Sidon into the hands of Persia, following the admission of a detachment within its walls. The defense then became impractical.

The two armies lay there for a while on the Pelusiac branch of the Nile, which was well protected by forts, fortified towns, and a network of canals on either side. He made no headway against the wall, for the garrison had built an additional barrier behind it. His attempts to breach the walls were thwarted by the presence of mobile towers that could be easily deployed to counter his attacks. The Egyptians, despite being defeated, maintained contact with Bagoas and agreed upon a specific date for him to bring his troops into the town.

Even sacred books, which he is said to have stolen from temples, may have been taken into account due to their value. The tribute is not said to have been increased. However, while all this is undoubtedly true, the end of Egypt might have been more dignified and honorable than it was.
